Adherent.title: Adherent
Adherent.description: Represents a member of the H2vl association, storing their personal and membership details.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Adherent entity.

nom.description: The last name of the association member.

prenom.description: The first name of the association member.

email.description: The email address of the association member, used for communication.

telephone.description: The phone number of the association member.

dateInscription.description: The date and time when the member's registration to the association was recorded.

Evenement.title: Evenement
Evenement.description: Represents an event organized by the H2vl association, including its details and cost.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Evenement entity.

titre.description: The title or name of the event.

description.description: A detailed description of the event, outlining its purpose and activities.

date.description: The date and time when the event is scheduled to take place.

lieu.description: The physical or virtual location where the event will be held.

prix.description: The price or cost required to attend the event.

Inscription.title: Inscription
Inscription.description: Records a member's registration for a specific event, including payment status.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Inscription entity.

evenementId.description: Reference to the Evenement entity this registration is associated with. (Relationship: Evenement 1:N Inscription)

adherentId.description: Reference to the Adherent entity who made this registration. (Relationship: Adherent 1:N Inscription)

aPaye.description: Indicates whether the payment for this event registration has been made. True if paid, false otherwise.

dateInscription.description: The date and time when the member registered for this specific event.

definition.description: Stores individual member profiles. The document ID (`adherentId`) directly corresponds to the Firebase Auth UID of the member. This ensures direct path-based ownership for security rules. Includes denormalized 'id' field matching 'adherentId' for consistency.

params.name: adherentId
params.description: The unique identifier for the Adherent, matching their Firebase Authentication UID.

definition.description: Contains details for all events organized by the association. This collection is publicly readable for event discovery. Write access is restricted to administrators only. The 'id' field within the document matches the 'evenementId' document ID.

params.name: evenementId
params.description: The unique identifier for the Evenement.

definition.description: Records a member's registration for a specific event. This is a subcollection under the respective Adherent's document. The 'adherentId' in the path must match the Firebase Auth UID of the requesting user. For Authorization Independence and QAPs, this document ideally includes denormalized 'titre', 'date', 'lieu', and 'prix' from the Evenement entity to avoid additional reads when listing a user's registrations. The 'id' field within the document matches the 'inscriptionId' document ID, and 'adherentId' field matches the parent 'adherentId' from the path.

params.name: adherentId
params.description: The unique identifier for the parent Adherent, matching their Firebase Authentication UID.

params.name: inscriptionId
params.description: The unique identifier for this specific event registration.

definition.description: A dedicated collection for Database Access Control (DBAC) to define administrators. The mere existence of a document at this path, where 'adminId' matches a user's Firebase Auth UID, grants administrative privileges. This uses an 'Existence over Content' model. While referring to the 'Adherent' schema for compliance, documents here are typically minimal (e.g., empty or containing only a timestamp) to denote admin status. Access to this collection itself is highly restricted to authorized systems for initial setup and management.

params.name: adminId
params.description: The Firebase Authentication UID of a user designated as an administrator.

firestore.reasoning:
The proposed Firestore data structure is designed to adhere strictly to the core design principles, especially Authorization Independence, QAPs (Queries Are Not Filters), and Structural Segregation, to ensure a secure, scalable, and easily debuggable backend for the H2vl association application. 

1.  **`/adherents/{adherentId}` Collection:** This collection stores the personal and membership details for each association member. The `adherentId` wildcard in the path directly corresponds to the Firebase Authentication `uid` of the logged-in user. This design choice enforces Path-Based ownership (Principle C.1, C.2), making security rules extremely simple: a user can only access their own `/adherents/{request.auth.uid}` document. This achieves Authorization Independence as the ownership is inherent in the path. For QAPs, a user attempting to list documents in `/adherents` will only be able to retrieve their own document based on their `uid`, effectively preventing unauthorized data access or filtering server-side. Administrators will have broader access to this collection, managed through a dedicated `/roles_admin` collection.

2.  **`/evenements/{evenementId}` Collection:** This collection houses the details of all events organized by the association. It is designed with a homogeneous security posture (Principle B): all events are publicly readable, allowing any user (authenticated or unauthenticated) to discover upcoming events (as per 'Event Discovery' requirement). Write access (create, update, delete) is restricted to administrators only. This clear segregation ensures simple security rules for listing and managing events. QAPs are fully supported, as a `list` operation will return all accessible events without requiring server-side filtering based on user permissions for general viewing.

3.  **`/adherents/{adherentId}/inscriptions/{inscriptionId}` Subcollection:** This subcollection is nested under the respective `/adherents/{adherentId}` document. This structure directly links an event registration to the member who made it (Principle C.2, C.3). The `adherentId` in the path matches the `request.auth.uid` for the registering user, thus providing immediate authorization context for any operation on their `inscription` documents. 
    
    **Authorization Independence via Denormalization (Mandate A) is critically applied here:** To prevent `get()` calls in security rules and facilitate efficient client-side data display (QAPs), key details from the related `Evenement` document (specifically `titre`, `date`, `lieu`, `prix`) are recommended to be denormalized directly into each `Inscription` document. This means when a user queries their `/adherents/{adherentId}/inscriptions` subcollection to see their registered events, they receive all necessary event information without needing to perform additional reads from the `/evenements` collection or requiring complex server-side joins. This significantly simplifies security rules for read operations on inscriptions, as all authorization context and display data are self-contained within the `inscription` document. The `adherentId` field within the `Inscription` document further reinforces the `adherentId` from the path for rule validation.

4.  **`/roles_admin/{adminId}` Collection:** This collection implements Database Access Control (DBAC - Principle C.3, C.5) by using the 'Existence over Content' pattern. A document's mere existence at `/roles_admin/{adminId}` where `adminId` is a user's `uid` signifies that user has administrative privileges. Security rules can then check `exists(/databases/$(database)/documents/roles_admin/$(request.auth.uid))` to grant elevated access to specific collections (like writing to `evenements` or managing all `adherents`). This avoids custom claims and keeps role management within Firestore, simplifying debugging and rule maintenance.

This architecture prioritizes clear ownership, predictable access patterns, and self-contained authorization contexts for each document, making the Firestore security rules robust, easy to write, and highly performant.
